description: The provided image is an irrelevant UI element that does not match the target materials science paper focused on fatigue properties of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel. It contains a logo composed of a blue-yellow segmented circular ring surrounding a red bookmark-shaped graphic, with the text "Check for updates" printed below. No valid figure information related to the paper's research content, including microstructures, mechanical property data, characterization results or experimental schematics, can be extracted from this mismatched image.

This schematic illustrates the geometry and dimensional specifications of flat dog-bone-shaped specimens used for uniaxial tensile and stress-controlled tension-tension fatigue tests of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel. The specimen has a total length of 55 mm, with two end clamping sections of 15 mm length and 7 mm width each, a 15 mm long parallel gauge section with 5 mm width, a transition fillet radius of 13 mm, and a uniform thickness of 2.5 mm. All specimens are machined via wire electrical discharge machining with their loading axis parallel to the rolling direction, followed by mechanical and electropolishing to eliminate surface stress and scratches. This standardized specimen design ensures consistent test conditions for evaluating the tensile and fatigue properties of the studied steel with different κ-carbide precipitation states.

This figure characterizes microstructures of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under different heat treatments. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) map of the solution-treated (ST) sample, showing equiaxed single-phase FCC austenite grains with a 100 μm scale bar. Subfigures (b), (c), (d) are bright-field TEM micrographs with corresponding [001] zone axis selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, for samples aged at 550℃ for 6 h (550-6), 600℃ for 6 h (600-6), and 650℃ for 6 h (650-6), respectively. (b) shows fine nanoscale intragranular κ-carbides with negligible intergranular precipitation (0.5 μm scale bar); (c) presents coarser intragranular κ-carbides plus minor intergranular κ-carbides (0.5 μm scale bar); (d) displays further coarsened intragranular κ-carbides and severe continuous intergranular κ-carbide precipitation (1 μm scale bar). It confirms that rising aging temperature increases κ-carbide size and promotes intergranular precipitation, which strongly influences the steel's fatigue properties.

This chart plots the average size of intragranular κ-carbide precipitates as a function of aging temperature for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel subjected to 6 h of isothermal aging, with data presented as mean ± standard deviation. The x-axis represents aging temperature ranging from 550 °C to 650 °C, while the y-axis measures intragranular κ-carbide size in nanometers. A clear monotonic increasing trend is observed: precipitate size rises from ~11 nm at 550 °C, to ~18 nm at 600 °C, up to ~24 nm at 650 °C, confirming that intragranular κ-carbide undergoes significant coarsening as aging temperature increases for a fixed 6 h aging duration. This quantifies the evolution of κ-carbide precipitation state, which is demonstrated to be a critical factor controlling the tensile and fatigue performance of the tested low-density steel, supporting the core conclusion that adjusting aging temperature tunes precipitate size to optimize mechanical properties.

This figure presents engineering tensile stress-strain curves of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples under four different heat treatment conditions: solution-treated (ST, green curve), aged at 550 ℃ for 6 h (550-6, black curve), aged at 600 ℃ for 6 h (600-6, red curve), and aged at 650 ℃ for 6 h (650-6, blue curve). The x-axis denotes engineering strain in %, while the y-axis denotes engineering stress in MPa. All aged samples exhibit significantly higher yield strength and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) but lower ductility compared with the ST sample. The 600-6 sample obtains the highest UTS of ~1100 MPa, followed by 550-6 (~1050 MPa), 650-6 (~970 MPa), and ST (~780 MPa). Elongation decreases from ~72% for ST to ~58% for 550-6 and 600-6, and ~53% for 650-6. These results support that intragranular κ-carbide precipitation delivers effective precipitation strengthening, while severe intergranular κ-carbide precipitation after 650 ℃ aging induces grain boundary weakening and reduces strength.

This figure presents the stress amplitude (Δσ/2, unit: MPa) versus number of reversals to fracture (2N_f, double logarithmic x-axis) fatigue curves for four Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples: ST (solution treated), 550-6 (aged at 550℃ for 6 h), 600-6 (aged at 600℃ for 6 h), and 650-6 (aged at 650℃ for 6 h). All curves show a linear relationship in the finite-life region under double-logarithmic coordinates, which fits the Basquin equation. All aged samples exhibit longer fatigue lives than the ST sample at a given stress amplitude. The fatigue strength (maximum stress amplitude sustained for 10^7 cycles without failure) is measured as 157.5 MPa for ST, 162 MPa for 550-6, 175.5 MPa for 600-6, and 171 MPa for 650-6. The 550-6 sample shows the longest fatigue life at higher stress amplitudes, while the 600-6 sample delivers the highest fatigue strength and longest life at low stress amplitudes near the fatigue limit. The result confirms that aging treatment effectively enhances fatigue resistance via κ-carbide precipitation, and the performance difference is attributed to the competition between intragranular precipitation strengthening and grain boundary weakening.

This set of 8 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents macroscopic fatigue fracture surfaces of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel. Subfigures (a)-(d) correspond to samples tested at 180 MPa stress amplitude, while (e)-(h) correspond to samples tested at 315 MPa stress amplitude. Each stress amplitude group includes four samples with different heat treatments: solution treated (ST: a, e), aged at 550 °C for 6 h (550-6: b, f), aged at 600 °C for 6 h (600-6: c, g), aged at 650 °C for 6 h (650-6: d, h). Yellow dashed lines mark fatigue crack propagation paths, each subfigure notes f_A (area fraction of the crack propagation zone) and carries a 500 μm scale bar. At 315 MPa stress amplitude, the ST sample exhibits a more tortuous crack path, while aged samples show distinct fracture features correlated with κ-carbide precipitation states. The figure demonstrates that κ-carbide precipitation state and stress amplitude jointly regulate fatigue fracture behavior, supporting the core conclusion that tailored κ-carbide precipitation improves fatigue performance of this low-density steel.

This group of 8 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents the fatigue crack propagation zone fracture surface morphologies of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under two stress amplitudes: 180 MPa (subfigures a-d) and 315 MPa (subfigures e-h). Subfigures a/e correspond to solution-treated (ST) samples, b/f to samples aged at 550 °C for 6 h (550-6), c/g to samples aged at 600 °C for 6 h (600-6), d/h to samples aged at 650 °C for 6 h (650-6). Each subfigure includes a low-magnification view (scale bar 100 μm) and a high-magnification inset (scale bar 10 μm) showing fatigue striation features. Obvious morphology differences are observed: fatigue striation spacing is larger under 315 MPa than 180 MPa; the 650-6 sample with severe intergranular κ-carbide precipitation shows partial intergranular fracture features, while the 600-6 sample has the finest striation spacing at 180 MPa, consistent with its highest fatigue limit. These results demonstrate that κ-carbide precipitation state (size, distribution, intergranular precipitation level) strongly regulates the fatigue fracture behavior of the studied steel.

This set of 8 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents the fracture surface morphologies of the rapid fracture zone for Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples under different heat treatments and fatigue stress amplitudes, with a uniform 50 μm scale bar for all subgraphs. Subgraphs a-d correspond to the stress amplitude Δσ/2 = 180 MPa, while subgraphs e-h correspond to Δσ/2 = 315 MPa. Subgraphs a/e are for the solution-treated (ST) sample, b/f for the sample aged at 550 °C for 6 h (550-6), c/g for the sample aged at 600 °C for 6 h (600-6), and d/h for the sample aged at 650 °C for 6 h (650-6). Visually, the rapid fracture zones of ST, 550-6, and 600-6 samples are dominated by dimples, indicating ductile fracture via microvoid coalescence. The 650-6 sample shows mixed fracture characteristics with both dimples and cleavage facets, corresponding to reduced ductility caused by extensive intergranular κ-carbide precipitation. This figure supports the conclusion that κ-carbide precipitation state critically controls the fatigue fracture mechanism of the studied steel.

This set of EBSD maps characterizes fatigue crack propagation paths of four Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples (solution treated ST, 550°C aged 550-6, 600°C aged 600-6, 650°C aged 650-6) tested at 225 MPa stress amplitude. For each sample: (a,d,g,j) are band contrast (BC) and inverse pole figure (IPF) superimposed maps showing grain orientation and crack morphology; (b,e,h,k) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps quantifying local plastic strain distribution; (c,f,i,l) are Schmid factor (SF) maps for {111}<110> slip systems. All samples exhibit transgranular crack propagation along slip bands. The ST sample has a relatively straight crack path and highest KAM intensity near cracks/grain boundaries indicating severe strain localization. Aged samples show more tortuous crack paths with frequent microbranching: 600-6 has the most diffuse KAM distribution (homogeneous plastic accommodation), while 650-6 shows KAM hot spots at intergranular κ-carbide decorated grain boundaries. SF maps confirm cracks nucleate in low-SF hard-oriented grains and propagate into high-SF soft-oriented grains. This figure supports that well-dispersed intragranular κ-carbides enhance fatigue crack propagation resistance via increased crack tortuosity and reduced strain localization, while excessive intergranular κ-carbides degrade performance.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images displays surface deformation morphologies of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel specimens after fatigue loading, with a uniform scale bar of 5 μm for all subfigures. Subfigure (a) shows the solution-treated (ST) sample fatigued at stress amplitude Δσ/2 = 180 MPa, where cross-slip induced wavy slip features are identified. Subfigure (b) presents the ST sample fatigued at Δσ/2 = 315 MPa, exhibiting severe localized deformation in the form of kink bands that indicate deteriorated deformability. Subfigures (c) and (d) correspond to the 600 °C aged for 6 h (600-6) sample fatigued at 180 MPa and 315 MPa respectively, both showing uniformly distributed planar slip bands, with decreased slip band spacing observed under higher stress amplitude. This figure confirms that intragranular κ-carbide precipitation in aged samples promotes reversible planar slip, mitigates catastrophic strain localization, and improves fatigue deformation performance compared to the solution-treated sample.

This set of 8 bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs presents cyclic deformation microstructures of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different heat treatments and fatigue loading conditions. Subfigures (a,b) correspond to solution-treated (ST) samples fatigued at Δσ/2 = 180 MPa and 315 MPa respectively, showing planar lamellar bands (PLBs) as dominant deformation features with 1 μm scale bars. Subfigures (c,d), (e,f), (g,h) correspond to 550-6 (aged at 550 °C for 6 h), 600-6 (aged at 600 °C for 6 h), 650-6 (aged at 650 °C for 6 h) samples respectively, each pair tested at Δσ/2 = 180 MPa and 315 MPa with 500 nm scale bars; all aged samples exhibit planar slip bands as the main deformation feature. Deformation band morphology evolves with aging temperature (controlling κ-carbide size and distribution) and stress amplitude, verifying that κ-carbide precipitation state directly modulates cyclic deformation mechanisms that underpin fatigue performance differences across samples.

This schematic figure compares the slip reversibility difference between solution-treated (ST) and aged Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples under cyclic loading with critical resolved shear stress τc. Subfigure (a) illustrates the ST condition: short-range ordering (SRO) structures exist in the austenitic matrix, and repeated dislocation shearing leads to premature, localized SRO destruction, inducing non-uniform softening and local strain concentration, which results in low slip reversibility. Subfigure (b) illustrates the aged condition: uniformly dispersed coherent L12-structured intragranular κ-carbides are present in the matrix. Dislocation shearing of these precipitates creates anti-phase boundaries and induces glide plane softening, promoting homogeneous planar slip band formation and distributing plastic strain evenly across multiple slip systems. This effect mitigates localized strain concentration, significantly improves dislocation motion reversibility under cyclic loading, explaining the superior fatigue performance of aged samples compared to ST samples.

This figure presents TEM analysis of dislocation-κ-carbide interactions in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples fatigued at stress amplitude Δσ/2 = 180 MPa, comparing 550-6 (aged at 550 ℃ for 6 h, with ~11 nm intragranular κ-carbides) and 650-6 (aged at 650 ℃ for 6 h, with ~24 nm intragranular κ-carbides) specimens. (a,b) are bright-field TEM images with 100 nm scale bar showing intragranular κ-carbides and dislocation lines in the two fatigued samples respectively. (c,d) are HRTEM images with 10 nm scale bar of κ-carbides and dislocations in the two samples; (c',d') are 5 nm scale magnified views of green-boxed regions in (c,d), and (c'',d'') are corresponding inverse fast Fourier transform filtered images with 5 nm scale, where κ-carbides, austenitic matrix, transition regions are marked as white, black, blue respectively. Red arrows in (c'',d'') indicate shear steps and ordered structure disruption (anti-phase boundaries) inside carbides. The observations confirm both sizes of κ-carbides are in the dislocation shearing regime, supporting that repeated dislocation shearing causes mechanical dissolution of κ-carbides during cyclic loading.

This set of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs presents deformation microstructures near grain boundaries of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel samples after fatigue testing at a stress amplitude Δσ/2 of 315 MPa. Subfigure (a) shows the 550-6 sample (aged at 550 °C for 6 h), where uniformly distributed fine intragranular κ-carbides facilitate the formation of organized planar slip bands, supporting relatively uniform slip transfer across grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) shows the 650-6 sample (aged at 650 °C for 6 h), where coarse intergranular κ-carbides precipitate at grain boundaries, acting as strong slip barriers to intensify dislocation pile-ups, induce local stress concentration, and finally lead to earlier crack nucleation and shorter fatigue life. The figure directly verifies the critical role of κ-carbide precipitation state in regulating fatigue deformation mechanisms of the studied steel.

This schematic diagram illustrates the slip band interaction with grain boundary (GB) in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under cyclic loading for fatigue property study. The left region represents soft-oriented grain, where abundant planar slip bands containing high density of dislocations (marked as T symbols) form as slip systems in this grain are favorably oriented for deformation. The right region represents hard-oriented grain, where slip systems are misoriented to accommodate slip transmission from the soft grain. The GB between the two grains blocks slip band motion from the soft-oriented grain, leading to dislocation pile-up at the GB as indicated by accumulated dislocations at the GB in the soft grain, and far fewer activated slip bands in the hard grain. This schematic supports the core conclusion that planar slip behavior, grain orientation mismatch and GB jointly influence fatigue damage accumulation, which is modulated by κ-carbide precipitation states in the studied steel.

This is a schematic illustration for cyclic deformation mechanism of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel, demonstrating the role of intergranular κ-carbides at grain boundary (GB) between soft-oriented grain and hard-oriented grain. It shows that intergranular κ-carbides block slip transmission across the grain boundary, intensify dislocation pile-ups originated from the soft-oriented grain at the GB, induce localized stress concentration, and thus accelerate fatigue damage under cyclic loading. This schematic explains the impact of κ-carbide precipitation state on fatigue properties of the studied steel, supporting the core conclusion that suppressing intergranular κ-carbide precipitation while promoting fine intragranular κ-carbide precipitation is an effective approach to improve the fatigue performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.
